audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Jerusalem council, circumcision dispute, Peter/James speeches, Spirit/grace, Gentile letter, Judas/Silas, Paul/Barnabas split.

verses.restored: Certain ones coming down from Judea were teaching the siblings, Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you are not able to be saved/healed.

After no small dissension and discussion happened between Paul and Barnabas and them, they appointed Paul and Barnabas and some others from them to go up to the apostles and elders in Jerusalem concerning this question.
Being sent on by the assembly, they were passing through Phoenicia and Samaria, describing the turning of the nations, and they were making great joy for all the siblings.
Arriving in Jerusalem, they were received by the assembly, apostles, and elders, and reported all that the Divine had done with them.
But some from the party of the Pharisees who had trusted rose, saying, It is necessary to circumcise them and command them to keep the Law of Moses.
The apostles and elders were gathered to see about this word.

After much discussion, Peter stood and said to them, Men, siblings, you know that from early days the Divine chose among you that through my mouth the nations should hear the word of the good news and trust.
The heart-knowing Divine bore witness to them, giving them the holy Spirit just as also to us,
and made no distinction between us and them, cleansing their hearts by trust.
Now therefore why do you test the Divine by placing a yoke upon the neck of the disciples that neither our fathers nor we were strong enough to bear?
But through the grace of the Lord Jesus we trust to be saved/healed in the same way as those also.
All the multitude became silent, and they were hearing Barnabas and Paul describe signs and wonders the Divine had done among the nations through them.
After they became silent, James answered, saying, Men, siblings, hear me.
Simeon described how the Divine first visited to take from the nations a people for his name.
With this the words of the prophets agree, just as it is written:
After these things I will return and rebuild the fallen tent of David; its ruins I will rebuild, and I will restore it,
so that the remnant of humans may seek the Lord, and all the nations upon whom my name has been called, says the Lord who does these things
known from the age.
Therefore I judge not to trouble those from the nations who are turning to the Divine,
but to write to them to abstain from pollutions of idols, sexual immorality, what is strangled, and blood.
For Moses from ancient generations has in every city those proclaiming him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath.

Then it seemed good to the apostles and elders, with the whole assembly, to choose men from them and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas: Judas called Barsabbas and Silas, leading men among the siblings,
writing by their hand: The apostles and elders, siblings, to the siblings from the nations in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: greetings.
Since we heard that some from us troubled you with words, unsettling your souls/lives, to whom we gave no command,
it seemed good to us, becoming of one accord, to choose men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,
humans who have handed over their lives/souls for the name of our Lord Jesus Messiah.
We have therefore sent Judas and Silas, and they also by word will report the same things.
For it seemed good to the holy Spirit and to us to lay upon you no greater burden except these necessary things:
to abstain from idol-offerings, blood, strangled things, and sexual immorality; keeping yourselves from these, you will do beautifully/well. Farewell.
So they, being released, went down to Antioch, and gathering the multitude, delivered the letter.
Reading it, they rejoiced over the encouragement.
Judas and Silas, also being prophets, encouraged and strengthened the siblings with many words.
After spending some time, they were released in peace from the siblings to those who had sent them.
[Some later manuscripts add that Silas decided to remain there.]
Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch, teaching and announcing the good news of the word of the Lord with many others also.
After some days Paul said to Barnabas, Let us return and visit the siblings in every city where we announced the word of the Lord, to see how they are.
Barnabas wanted to take along John called Mark.
But Paul considered it not fitting to take along this one who had withdrawn from them from Pamphylia and had not gone with them to the work.
A sharp disagreement happened, so that they separated from one another. Barnabas, taking Mark, sailed away to Cyprus.
But Paul, choosing Silas, went out, being handed over to the grace of the Lord by the siblings.
He was passing through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the assemblies.
